steps.message:
<uploaded_files>/app</uploaded_files>

# Forum digest: "A few statement rows get the wrong category or payee"

Collected from a user support thread. In every case the money moved correctly;
only the derived label is wrong - the category, the sub-tag, or the counterparty
name - and only on a handful of unusual row shapes. Three representative posts,
lightly anonymized, then a maintainer note.

---

**mango_saver** -
> Two rows on this month's statement look off. A credit whose reference note is
> nothing but a long all-numeric string used to show up as a cheque deposit and
> now it just reads as a generic transfer. Separately, my salary credit - whose
> narration is a bare numeric code and nothing else - has stopped picking up the
> salary sub-tag it always had.

**rk_ontheroad** -
> Mine is the name field, not the category. On transfers whose narration opens
> with a direction word ahead of the name, the counterparty now comes back
> empty or wrong. Ordinary transfers that don't open that way are still
> labelled fine; it's only that leading-keyword narration style.

**auditlens** -
> Bill transfers that use a slash-delimited reference are pulling the wrong
> payee. When the reference is laid out as four fields - a type, a sub-type, a
> numeric reference, then the payee name as the final field - the app is lifting
> the numeric reference into the name column instead of the actual name sitting
> at the end.

---

**Maintainer note.** Consolidating these. Each one sits on an edge form that
everyday rows rarely produce, which is why routine activity looks healthy and
nothing tripped in review. On top of the three above we reproduced one more in
the same family: an offsetting credit that arrives immediately after its
matching debit is being recorded as a bounce rather than the reversal it
really is.

The labeling logic is deterministic - the same row always maps to the same
label - so each complaint can be recreated from a single synthetic row and
chased to the exact rule at fault. Expect the faults to sit on boundaries and
structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules, not in the common paths.

**Ask for engineering:** restore correct handling so these edge rows label
correctly again, without shifting any row that already comes out right. Success
is that the situations described here enrich correctly and nothing that is
currently correct changes.

steps.message: The Controller pipeline runs: bank categorization → purpose → clean_names → internal_payment → salary_detection → pfm → simple_description. Let me investigate the relevant utility scripts and find the edge-case bugs.
steps.reasoning_content:
This is a Flask app. The pipeline is: bank categorization → purpose → clean_names → internal_payment → salary_detection → pfm_categorization → simple_description.

The four bugs are about edge cases:
1. Cheque deposit credit (long all-numeric reference) now reads as generic transfer — was "cheque deposit" category
2. Salary credit (bare numeric code narration) stopped getting the salary sub-tag
3. Transfers whose narration opens with a direction word ahead of name — counterparty name empty/wrong
4. Bill transfers with slash-delimited reference (4 fields: type/sub-type/numeric-ref/payee-name) — lifting numeric ref as payee instead of name at end
5. (maintainer) offsetting credit immediately after matching debit recorded as bounce instead of reversal

Let me investigate. The clues mention:
- "boundaries and structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules"
- These are about regex parsing in the BankScripts or UtilityScripts

Let me explore. The key candidates: InternalPayment (bounce/reversal), SalaryDetection (salary sub-tag), CleanName (counterparty), Purpose (category), and bank scripts.

Let me look at the utility scripts and a representative bank script. Given "bill transfers that use a slash-delimited reference ... four fields - a type, a sub-type, a numeric reference, then the payee name as the final field", this sounds like it's in some bank script that parses narration by splitting on '/'.

Let me start with InternalPayment for the reversal/bounce issue.

1: import pandas as pd
2: import numpy as np
3: from scipy.spatial.distance import pdist, squareform

14: 
15: 
16: def salary_detection(transactions):
17:     try:
18:         #assign empty string to cells with null values
19:         transactions['partyName'] = transactions['partyName'].fillna("")
20: 
21:         #grouping of data by similar party names
22:         STOP_WORDS_PARTYNAME = "privatelimited|private|limited|pvtltd"
23:         transactions['party2'] = transactions['partyName'].replace(to_replace=" ", value="", regex=True).str.lower().replace(to_replace=STOP_WORDS_PARTYNAME, value="", regex=True).str.strip()
24:         # grouping using clustering
25:         distance_matrix = squareform(pdist(transactions['party2'].values.reshape(-1,1), lambda x, y : distance(x[0], y[0])))
26:         
27:         # For testing purpose only
28:         # dend = dendrogram(linkage(distance_matrix, method  = "complete"))
29:         # plt.title('Dendrogram')
30:         # plt.xlabel('Customers')
31:         # plt.ylabel('Euclidean distances')
32:         # plt.show()
33:         
34:         transactions['Group_ID'] = cut_tree(linkage(distance_matrix, method='complete'), height=1.5)
35: 
36:         #finding the probable salary txns set
37:         NOT_SALARY = "XX4700.*Mobi|Mobi.*XX4700|credit card/|Deposit / Investment|MOB/TPFT/"
38:         LOAN_POSITIVE = "\\binsta\\s?loan|SHUBHLOANS|DISBUR-|\\besloan|\\bloan\\b|disbur\\s?s|disbur|disbu\\b|cashbean fund tr|NODAL A/C|NODAL Ac|NODALAC|KisshtFund|NODAL A C|ICICI BANK NODA"
39:         EXCLUDE_DEPOSIT = "CASH_DEPOSIT|FIXED_DEPOSIT|SELF_DEPOSIT|CASH_DEPOSIT_CHARGES|DEPOSITORY_CHARGES|FLEXI_DEPOSIT|INTEREST_ON_DEPOSIT|RECURRING_DEPOSIT"   # All the deposits except cheque deposit will be excluded from salary txn
40:        
41:         # filtering out txns to create sub txn dataframe for salary detection
42:         transactions_sub = transactions[transactions['type'].eq("CREDIT") & transactions['description'].notnull() & transactions['amount'].gt(2999)]
43:         transactions_sub = transactions_sub[~transactions_sub['transactionSubcategory'].isin(["INTERNAL_PAYMENT", "INVESTMENT_INCOME", "TRANSFER_FROM_WALLET", "EMI_MORATORIUM", "RENT", "REIMBURSEMENT", "SUBSIDIES", "RENT_INCOME"])]
44:         transactions_sub = transactions_sub[~transactions_sub['merchantType'].isin(["Pension"])]
45:         transactions_sub = transactions_sub[~transactions_sub['transactionCategory'].isin(["CASHBACK", "AUTO_PAYMENT_BOUNCE", "BANK_ACCOUNT_CLOSURE", "UPI", "IMPS","AUTO_SWEEP"])]
46:         transactions_sub = transactions_sub[~transactions_sub['transactionCategory'].str.contains("REVERSAL|RETURN|INTEREST|REFUND|CANCEL", na=False)]
47:         transactions_sub = transactions_sub[~transactions_sub['transactionCategory'].str.contains(EXCLUDE_DEPOSIT, na = False, case=False)]
48:         transactions_sub = transactions_sub[~transactions_sub['description'].str.contains(LOAN_POSITIVE, na=False, case=False)]
49:         transactions_sub = transactions_sub[~transactions_sub['description'].str.contains(NOT_SALARY, na=False, case=False)]
50:         transactions_sub = transactions_sub[transactions_sub['partyName'].str.len().gt(0) & transactions_sub['partyName'].notnull()]
51: 
52:         # remove txns with salary amount more than 30000 and divisible by 10000
53:         transactions_sub = transactions_sub[~(transactions_sub['amount'].gt(30000) & transactions['amount'].mod(10000).eq(0))]
54:         
55:         #transforming date into YYYY-MM form
56:         transactions['dateSal'] = pd.to_datetime(transactions['transactionDate'], format="%d/%m/%Y").dt.to_period('M')
57:         # calculate month_freq
58:         month_freq = len(transactions.groupby('dateSal'))
59: 
60:         #Find whether salary detected or not
61:         salaryDetect = "SALARY" in transactions.transactionSubcategory.values
62: 
63:         # Scenario 1 - when salary detect is FALSE
64:         if salaryDetect == False and len(transactions_sub) > 0:
65:             #CASE 1.1 - Assigning salary based on same amount repeating more than 1
66:             mode_amount = transactions_sub.amount.mode().max()
67:             mode_freq = transactions_sub.amount.value_counts().max()
68:             if mode_freq > 1:
69:                 mode_group_id = transactions_sub[transactions_sub['amount'].eq(mode_amount)].iloc[0].Group_ID
70:                 conditions_choices = [

72:                 ]

82:                     if name_freq >= 0.5 * month_freq:

87:                     ]
88:                     df = pd.DataFrame(data=conditions_choices, columns=['conditions', 'choices'])
89:                     transactions['transactionSubcategory'] = np.select(df['conditions'], df['choices'], default=transactions.transactionSubcategory)
90:             #Scenario 1.3 - Again detecting the salary from the transaction not having partyName, so that frequent salary are same

steps.reasoning_content:
Salary detection needs partyName. The user "mango_saver" says: salary credit whose narration is a bare numeric code and nothing else has stopped picking up the salary sub-tag. This suggests that when partyName is empty (no name extracted), the salary detection skips it. But the maintainer note says "faults sit on boundaries and structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules" - so it's a regex bug somewhere, not a heuristic issue. The salary narration is a "bare numeric code" - and salary_detection requires `partyName.str.len().gt(0)`. So if partyName is empty (because clean_names failed to extract the name from a bare numeric narration), salary won't be detected. So this is downstream of a clean_name failure.

Actually let me reconsider. The complaints:
1. mango_saver: cheque deposit credit (long all-numeric reference note) used to show as cheque deposit, now generic transfer. AND salary credit (bare numeric code narration) stopped getting salary sub-tag.
2. rk_ontheroad: transfers whose narration opens with a direction word ahead of the name — counterparty empty/wrong.
3. auditlens: bill transfers with slash-delimited reference (4 fields: type/sub-type/numeric-ref/payee-name) — lifting numeric ref as payee instead of name at end.
4. maintainer: offsetting credit immediately after matching debit recorded as bounce rather than reversal.

These all relate to parsing narration to extract partyName or to categorize. Let me look at CleanName and Purpose, and the BankRegexConstants / RegexConstants.

steps.reasoning_content:
CleanName is a big lookup table. The bug about "transfers whose narration opens with a direction word ahead of the name" counterparty coming back empty/wrong, and "bill transfers that use a slash-delimited reference ... four fields" — these are likely in the BankScripts that parse narration/description to populate partyName. Let me look at the RegexConstants and BankRegexConstants, and at the bank scripts. Since the issue references generic terms, the bugs likely live in a shared utility or in a specific bank script. Let me look at the RegexConstants and a couple of bank scripts like HDFC/ICICI.

Actually, the maintainer note says "Expect the faults to sit on boundaries and structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules". This suggests specific regex patterns with off-by-one errors (e.g., capturing group index wrong). Let me look at RegexConstants and BankRegexConstants.

steps.reasoning_content:
The mango_saver complaint: "A credit whose reference note is nothing but a long all-numeric string used to show up as a cheque deposit and now it just reads as a generic transfer." 

Looking at YES.py line 53 and UCO.py line 45: `transactions.remark.str.contains("^[0-9]{3,6}$", ...)` — these match a remark that is a 3-6 digit number. The complaint says "long all-numeric string" — that's longer than 6 digits! So the regex `^[0-9]{3,6}$` is bounded by max 6 digits. A "long all-numeric string" (e.g., 11+ digits like the HDFC one `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` for remark len 15). Hmm.

Wait, "boundaries and structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules" — the bug is a bound that excludes the long numeric. For YES.py/UCO.py, the pattern is `^[0-9]{3,6}$`. A "long all-numeric string" (say 15 digits) would NOT match because of the {3,6} upper bound. So the fix would be to extend the upper bound. But which bank? The complaint says "used to show up as a cheque deposit and now it just reads as a generic transfer" — meaning a regression. So something changed that reduced the range. HDFC.py line 124:

```
[transactions.transactionCategory.eq("TRANSFER") & transactions.description.str.contains("^[0-9]{6}$", na=False, case=False) & transactions.type.eq("CREDIT") & (accountType=="SAVINGS") , "SALARY"],
```

This is the salary sub-tag for HDFC. It matches a description that is EXACTLY 6 digits (`^[0-9]{6}$`). The complaint: "my salary credit - whose narration is a bare numeric code and nothing else - has stopped picking up the salary sub-tag it always had."

A "bare numeric code" — but the rule requires exactly 6 digits. If the salary narration is a numeric code of a different length (not exactly 6, e.g., 7 or 8 digits), it won't match. "Boundaries and structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules" — the `{6}` exact bound is the boundary. The salary narration is a bare numeric code, but its length isn't exactly 6. So `^[0-9]{6}$` fails.

So the fix: change `^[0-9]{6}$` to `^[0-9]{6,}$` or similar to allow longer numeric codes. But wait — "bare numeric code and nothing else" — it should be purely digits. The rule already checks transactionCategory == "TRANSFER" (so it's the default category for unmatched). And requires CREDIT and SAVINGS. The only boundary issue is the exact `{6}`.

Hmm but this is HDFC-specific. The complaint "salary credit whose narration is a bare numeric code" — HDFC salary credits often have narration like a 6-digit code. But the user says it "stopped" — regression. If originally `^[0-9]{6,}$` and got changed to `^[0-9]{6}$`? Or the salary code length changed. The fix: allow variable length numeric, e.g. `^[0-9]+$` with a minimum.

But careful: "nothing that is currently correct changes." Currently a 6-digit numeric TRANSFER credit → SALARY (correct, keep). A non-6-digit numeric (e.g. 7) → currently TRANSFER (the bug). Changing to `^[0-9]{6,}$` would catch 6+ digits. But what about a 5-digit? Would a 5-digit numeric currently be TRANSFER (not salary)? The complaint implies the salary code is "bare numeric code" — could be 7-8 digits. Now I have a strong picture for HDFC. Let me analyze the bugs within HDFC:

**Bug 1 (cheque deposit, mango_saver):** Line 100:
```
[(transactions.remark.str.len().eq(15)) & transactions.type.eq("CREDIT") & transactions.remark.str.contains("^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}", na=False, case=False) , "CHEQUE_DEPOSIT"]
```
This is the cheque deposit detection for a credit whose reference note (remark) is a "long all-numeric string". The bug: `remark.str.len().eq(15)` requires exactly length 15, AND `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` requires 11 leading zeros + 5 digits = 16 chars actually! Wait, `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` matches 11 zeros followed by 5 digits = 16 characters. But `len().eq(15)` requires 15. These contradict! A 15-char remark can't match a 16-char pattern. So this rule NEVER fires for a 15-char remark.

Wait, let me recount: `[0]{11}` = 11 zeros, `[0-9]{5}` = 5 digits → total 16 chars matched by the regex. But the length check is `eq(15)`. So the regex requires at least 16 chars, but length must be exactly 15. Contradiction → never matches. 

Hmm, but the complaint says it "used to show up as a cheque deposit". So previously this worked. Maybe the length bound should be 16, or the pattern should be `[0]{11}[0-9]{4}` (15 chars: 11+4). 

A "long all-numeric string" — 15 or 16 digits. If the remark is 16 digits with 11 leading zeros, then `len().eq(15)` fails. If the remark is 15 digits with 11 leading zeros, then pattern `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` needs 16 → fails. So either way it fails. The "boundary" issue: the length check `eq(15)` vs the pattern length 16 mismatch.

The correct fix: either change `eq(15)` to `eq(16)`, or change `[0-9]{5}` to `[0-9]{4}`. Which is the "structural offset"? The pattern `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` = 16 chars. So length should be 16. But the complaint says "long all-numeric string" — could be 16 digits. Let me think: HDFC cheque deposit reference (CBS ref) is typically 16 digits: 11 zeros + 5 digit cheque number = 16. So `len().eq(15)` is off by one — should be `eq(16)`. That's the "structural offset" — the length bound is one short.

Hmm, but actually the pattern `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` — does the `$` anchor matter? steps.reasoning_content:
Now, the auditlens bug: "Bill transfers that use a slash-delimited reference are pulling the wrong payee. When the reference is laid out as four fields - a type, a sub-type, a numeric reference, then the payee name as the final field - the app is lifting the numeric reference into the name column instead of the actual name sitting at the end."

"Bill transfers" = BILL_PAYMENT category. In ICICI, line 54: `^BIL/|BPAY/|BBPS|Bill Payment` & DEBIT → "BILL_PAYMENT". The description is slash-delimited with four fields: type/sub-type/numeric-ref/payee-name. E.g., "BIL/BPAY/123456/John Doe". The partyName extraction should take the last field (index 3), but it's taking the numeric reference (index 2).

Look at line 148: `^BIL/BPAY.*BBPS|^BIL/BBPS.*BBPS|^BIL/INFT|^BIL/NEFT` → sep_by_(description,4,5). sep_by_(desc,4,5) takes elements [4:5] = the 5th field (0-indexed 4). Wait sep_by_ uses `.str[n1:n2]` so `sep_by_(desc,4,5)` = element index 4. For "BIL/BPAY/.../..." — fields: 0=BIL, 1=BPAY, 2=..., 3=..., 4=name. So index 4 = name. Hmm.

But the complaint: "four fields - a type, a sub-type, a numeric reference, then the payee name as the final field". So fields: [0]=type, [1]=sub-type, [2]=numeric reference, [3]=payee name. The app lifts index 2 (numeric reference) instead of index 3 (name).

So the rule should extract index 3 (the 4th field, 0-indexed 3), but it's extracting index 2.

Which line in ICICI handles "BIL/" with 4 fields? Let me see:
- Line 147: `^BIL/INFT/` & count("/").eq(3) → sep_by_(desc,2,3). count("/")=3 means 4 fields (split by / gives 4 parts). Fields: [0]=BIL, [1]=INFT, [2]=..., [3]=name. sep_by_(desc,2,3) = index 2. But the name is at index 3! So this lifts index 2 (which would be the numeric reference) instead of index 3 (name). 

Wait, count("/").eq(3): "BIL/INFT/123456/John" has 3 slashes → 4 fields: BIL, INFT, 123456, John. The name is at index 3. But the rule does sep_by_(desc,2,3) = index 2 = "123456" (numeric reference). That matches the complaint EXACTLY: "four fields - a type, a sub-type, a numeric reference, then the payee name as the final field - the app is lifting the numeric reference into the name column instead of the actual name sitting at the end."

So line 147: `^BIL/INFT/` with 3 slashes (4 fields) → should extract index 3, but extracts index 2. The "structural offset": off-by-one. Fix: `sep_by_(transactions.description,3,4)`.

Wait, but let me double check the field layout. "BIL/INFT/123456/John Doe":
- split('/') = ['BIL', 'INFT', '123456', 'John Doe']
- index 0 = 'BIL' (type)
- index 1 = 'INFT' (sub-type)
- index 2 = '123456' (numeric reference)
- index 3 = 'John Doe' (payee name)

The complaint says app lifts the numeric reference (index 2). The rule line 147 does sep_by_(desc,2,3) = index 2 = '123456'. Yes! That's the bug. Fix → sep_by_(desc,3,4) = index 3 = name.

But hold on — is line 147 the right one? Let me re-read line 147: `[transactions.description.str.contains("^BIL/INFT/", na=False, case=False) & transactions.description.str.count("/").eq(3)  , sep_by_(transactions.description,2,3)]`. Yes. The condition: starts with "BIL/INFT/" AND has exactly 3 slashes (4 fields). And it takes index 2. But the name is at index 3. Off-by-one.

But wait — is "BIL/INFT/123456/John" really the format? "BIL" = type (Bill), "INFT" = sub-type, "123456" = numeric reference, "John" = payee. Yes. Four fields. The rule takes index 2 (numeric ref). Should take index 3. Fix.

But careful: the maintainer note says "without shifting any row that currently comes out right." Currently rows matching line 147 get index 2 (wrong, numeric). So fixing to index 3 changes them to the name. Are there currently-correct rows that rely on index 2? If some "BIL/INFT/" with 3 slashes have the name at index 2 (not index 3)... but the complaint explicitly says the name is the final field (index 3). So index 2 is always the numeric ref for this 4-field format. Fixing is correct.

Hmm, but actually — is auditlens an ICICI user? "Bill transfers that use a slash-delimited reference" — ICICI uses "BIL/..." format heavily. Yes. And the four-field layout matches line 147's `count("/").eq(3)`.

But wait, there's also line 154: `^INF/INFT/` & count("/").eq(4) → sep_by_(desc,4,5). That's 5 fields, name at index 4. That seems right (index 4 for 5 fields). So that one is correct. The bug is line 147 using index 2 for a 4-field (name at index 3).

Actually wait — let me reconsider. Line 147 says count("/").eq(3) → 4 fields → name at index 3. But it takes index 2. So off by one. The correct: index 3 → sep_by_(desc,3,4). Yes.

But hmm, is the "sub-type" really INFT? "BIL/INFT/.../..." — fields: BIL, INFT, <num>, <name>. So type=BIL, sub-type=INFT, numeric ref, name. The complaint: "type, a sub-type, a numeric reference, then the payee name". Matches: BIL (type), INFT (sub-type), numeric, name. 

So fix line 147: `sep_by_(transactions.description,2,3)` → `sep_by_(transactions.description,3,4)`.

Now bug 3 (rk_ontheroad): "On transfers whose narration opens with a direction word ahead of the name, the counterparty now comes back empty or wrong." 

"transfers" → category TRANSFER. "narration opens with a direction word ahead of the name". Direction word... in ICICI? Let me look at the TRANSFER handling in ICICI. Line 159: `TRFR TO:|TRFR FROM:` → py_extract(desc, "TRFR (TO|FROM):(.*)", index=0). Line 160: `^TRF/` → sep_by_(desc,1,2). Line 161: `^CLG/(?!(CHQ|CLG))` → sep_by_(desc,1,2). 

Hmm, "direction word ahead of the name" — maybe "TRFR TO:" or "TRFR FROM:". Or maybe a bank where transfers open with "To"/"From"/"By".

Actually, "direction word" — could be literally a word indicating direction: "TO" or "FROM". Narration opens with a direction word ahead of the name: e.g., "TO RAJESH" or "FROM RAJESH".

Which bank handles TRANSFER with leading "To"/"From"? Let me search the bank scripts for transfer partyName with "From"/"To" leading. Actually, this is in HDFC line 256 (`^Trn?f To |^Fnf To |^Paid To |^Fund Trf To|^Amt Paid To` → extract " To (.*)"). And HDFC line 253 (TRANSFER & "^By" → strip "By "). 

But "direction word ahead of the name" — these HDFC rules handle "To <name>" / "By <name>". But the complaint says counterparty comes back "empty or wrong" for that leading-keyword style. So one of these rules is broken.

Hmm, but which bank is rk_ontheroad? Let me reconsider. Actually let me search broadly for transfer partyName extraction with direction words across bank scripts. Let me look at SBI, Axis, Kotak, etc. for TRANSFER handling.

Actually, let me reconsider the whole thing. The maintainer says "faults sit on boundaries and structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules". Bug 3 is a partyName extraction bug for transfers opening with a direction word. Let me look at the bank scripts that have TRANSFER category and extract names from "To/From/By" prefixes.

Let me grep for "TRANSFER" partyName patterns with From/To. Actually, let me look at the HDFC transfer rules again. Line 200: `TRANSFER & count("-") in [1,2] & ~contains("/|:|^RAZORPAY |-P-|RENTAL") & contains("^[A-Z]")` → sep_by_dash(desc,0,1). This takes the part BEFORE the first dash. For a description like "TO RAJESH-12345", sep_by_dash(0,1) = "TO RAJESH". Hmm that includes "TO".

Hmm, let me reconsider. Actually maybe bug 3 is in a different bank. Let me think about which bank has a clear "direction word ahead of the name" transfer pattern with an off-by-one.

Let me look at the other bank scripts quickly: SBI, Axis, Kotak, Canara, BOB, PNB. Let me grep for transfer-related partyName patterns.

Actually, the most distinctive phrasing: "transfers whose narration opens with a direction word ahead of the name". And "Ordinary transfers that don't open that way are still labelled fine; it's only that leading-keyword narration style." So there's a specific rule for transfers that open with a keyword (direction word), and it's broken. Ordinary transfers (no leading keyword) use a different rule that works.

This strongly maps to HDFC line 200 vs line 201:
- Line 200: TRANSFER & count("-") in [1,2] & ... & contains("^[A-Z]") → sep_by_dash(0,1) (before first dash). This is the "ordinary" transfer rule (handles "NAME-12345" → "NAME").
- Line 201: TRANSFER/REVERSAL & count("/").eq(1) → sep_by_(desc,1,2) (after first slash). 

Hmm, these don't match "direction word".

Let me look at HDFC line 256 again: `[partyName.contains("^Trn?f To |^Fnf To |^Paid To |^Fund Trf To|^Amt Paid To") , py_extract(partyName, " To (.*)", index=0)]`. This is a "clean name" step that strips "To " and extracts the name after "To". And line 253: `[TRANSFER & partyName.contains("^By") , partyName.replace("^By ", "")]`.

But these operate on partyName (already extracted), not directly on description. "direction word ahead of the name" — "To"/"By" are direction words. If a transfer's narration is "Trnf To Rajesh Kumar", the partyName extraction... Let me trace.

Actually, I'm now doubting HDFC for bug 3. Let me look at other banks. Let me check SBI and Axis transfer handling, since "direction word" might be "To/From/By".

Let me search for transfer partyName with leading keywords across banks.

Lots of candidates. The complaint: "transfers whose narration opens with a direction word ahead of the name, the counterparty now comes back empty or wrong. Ordinary transfers that don't open that way are still labelled fine; it's only that leading-keyword narration style."

Let me focus on banks that have a clear "leading-keyword" rule vs an "ordinary" rule. Andhra lines 75-77 have `^T[Oo]`, `^TRF TO `, `^TRF FRM `. These extract `^T[Oo] (.*)`. "direction word ahead of the name" — "TO"/"TRF TO"/"TRF FRM". Hmm.

Actually "direction word" — singular — suggests ONE direction word. Like "To" or "From" or "By". Let me reconsider the HDFC line 256: `^Trn?f To |^Fnf To |^Paid To |^Fund Trf To|^Amt Paid To` → `py_extract(partyName, " To (.*)", index=0)`. This strips " To " and takes the rest as the name. "direction word = To". The name comes after. If partyName = "Trnf To Rajesh Kumar", extract " To (.*)" → "Rajesh Kumar". That works...

Hmm, but the complaint says it comes back "empty or wrong." Let me think about which rule is off.

Actually, let me reconsider the Axis bank. Line 146: TRANSFER & count("/").eq(2) → sep_by_(desc,0,1). Line 147: TRANSFER & count("/").eq(1) → sep_by_(desc,0,1). Line 148: TRANSFER & len>3 & ~contains(special/num) → desc. 

Hmm Axis lines 146/147 take index 0 (first field before slash). For a transfer narration opening with a direction word... no.

Let me reconsider. "narration opens with a direction word ahead of the name". Let me look at BOB, BOI, etc. Actually, the phrase "direction word" is unusual. In Indian banking narrations, common leading direction words: "TO", "FROM", "BY", "TRF TO", "TRF FRM". 

Let me look at Andhra more — lines 75-77 are exactly "leading keyword narration style": `^T[Oo] `, `^TRF TO `, `^TRF FRM `. And there's presumably an ordinary transfer rule too. Let me read Andhra.py fully.

sep_by_dash(desc,0,1) = part before first dash. For "TRANSFER TO 12345678901-RAMESH", sep_by_dash(0,1) = "TRANSFER TO 12345678901", then replace "TRANSFER TO [0-9]{11}" → "" (empty!). So the name (after the dash) is lost → empty! That matches "comes back empty"!

steps.reasoning_content:
SBI lines 131-132:
```
[TRANSFER & contains("^TRANSFER TO ") , sep_by_dash(desc,0,1).replace("TRANSFER TO [0-9]{11}","")]
[TRANSFER & contains("^TRANSFER FROM ") , sep_by_dash(desc,0,1).replace("TRANSFER FROM [0-9]{11}","")]
```
The narration: "TRANSFER TO 12345678901 RAMESH KUMAR" — wait, SBI transfer narration is like "TRANSFER TO <11-digit account>-<name>"? Or "TRANSFER TO <name>"? 

Actually SBI NEFT/RTGS narrations: "TO TRANSFER-..." Hmm. Let me think about the format "TRANSFER TO <acct>-<name>" or "TRANSFER TO <name>".

The rule takes sep_by_dash(desc,0,1) = everything before the first dash, then replaces "TRANSFER TO [0-9]{11}" (the keyword + 11-digit account number) with empty. So if the narration is "TRANSFER TO 12345678901-RAMESH KUMAR", sep_by_dash(0,1) = "TRANSFER TO 12345678901", replace → "". Empty! The name "RAMESH KUMAR" is in the part AFTER the dash (index 1). So the rule should take sep_by_dash(1,2) for the name. That's the off-by-one → "comes back empty."

But wait — is that the "leading-keyword narration style"? "TRANSFER TO " is a leading keyword (direction word "TO"). And "ordinary transfers that don't open that way are still labelled fine" — other TRANSFER rules (line 130, 148) handle dash-separated narrations by taking index 0 (the name part). 

Hmm, but actually let me reconsider the SBI format. SBI transfer narration for IMPS/NEFT might be "TRANSFER TO <name>". But the rule replaces "TRANSFER TO [0-9]{11}" — implying the format is "TRANSFER TO <11-digit number> <name>" or "TRANSFER TO <11-digit number>-<name>".

If format is "TRANSFER TO 12345678901 RAMESH KUMAR" (space-separated, no dash): sep_by_dash(0,1) = whole string (no dash) = "TRANSFER TO 12345678901 RAMESH KUMAR", replace "TRANSFER TO [0-9]{11}" → " RAMESH KUMAR" → strip → "RAMESH KUMAR". That works! 

If format is "TRANSFER TO 12345678901-RAMESH KUMAR" (dash-separated): sep_by_dash(0,1) = "TRANSFER TO 12345678901", replace → "". Empty. Bug.

So whether it's a bug depends on the exact format. The complaint says "comes back empty or wrong" — consistent with the dash format producing empty. 

Hmm, this is plausible but I'm not certain about SBI. Let me reconsider whether SBI is rk_ontheroad's bank.

Actually, let me reconsider the whole mapping. I've been assuming specific banks. But the maintainer said "Each complaint can be recreated from a single synthetic row and chased to the exact rule at fault." and "faults sit on boundaries and structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules."

Let me re-examine. The clearest "structural offset" bugs I've found:
1. HDFC line 100: `len().eq(15)` vs pattern `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` (16 chars) — off-by-one. Cheque deposit. ✓ (matches mango_saver bug 1)
2. HDFC line 124: `^[0-9]{6}$` exact — boundary too tight. Salary. ✓ (matches mango_saver bug 2)
3. HDFC line 98: `description.eq(description.shift(periods=2))` should be shift(1) — offset. Reversal/bounce. ✓ (matches maintainer bug 5)
4. ICICI line 147: `sep_by_(desc,2,3)` should be `sep_by_(desc,3,4)` — off-by-one. Bill transfer 4-field. ✓ (matches auditlens bug 4)
5. Bug 3 (rk_ontheroad direction-word transfer): TBD.

So bugs 1,2,5 are HDFC; bug 4 is ICICI. Bug 3 could be HDFC or another bank. Given the pattern that bugs cluster per-bank per user, and rk_ontheroad is a different user, bug 3 could be in any bank.

Let me reconsider HDFC for bug 3. HDFC line 201: `[TRANSFER/REVERSAL & count("/").eq(1) , sep_by_(desc,1,2)]`. This takes the field after the first slash. For a narration with one slash like "TO/RAJESH" → sep_by_(desc,1,2) = "RAJESH". Hmm, that works. But "direction word ahead of the name" with a slash? "TO/RAJESH"? The direction word "TO" is before the slash, name after. sep_by_(1,2) takes index 1 = name. Works.

But what about HDFC line 256: `^Trn?f To |^Fnf To |^Paid To |^Fund Trf To|^Amt Paid To` → `py_extract(partyName, " To (.*)", index=0)`. If partyName = "Trnf To Rajesh Kumar", extract " To (.*)" → "Rajesh Kumar". Works. But the complaint says "empty or wrong." 

Hmm. Let me reconsider HDFC line 200 (ordinary transfer): `TRANSFER & count("-") in [1,2] & ~contains("/|:|^RAZORPAY |-P-|RENTAL") & contains("^[A-Z]")` → sep_by_dash(desc,0,1). Takes before first dash. This is the "ordinary" rule. Works for "NAME-12345" → "NAME".

Where's the leading-keyword transfer rule in HDFC? Line 256 handles "Trnf To ", "Paid To " etc. in the clean step. But the first-pass extraction for a transfer whose narration is "Trnf To Rajesh Kumar" (no dash, no slash)... line 200 requires count("-") in [1,2], so no dash → line 200 doesn't fire. Line 201 requires count("/")=1 → no. Line 214/215 require remark numeric. So "Trnf To Rajesh Kumar" (pure text) wouldn't match any first-pass rule → partyName stays "". Then clean step line 256 operates on partyName (empty) → no match → stays "". So counterparty empty! 

Hmm, that's a different kind of bug (missing rule, not offset). The maintainer says faults are offsets in otherwise-correct rules, not missing rules. So bug 3 is more likely an existing rule with an off-by-one.

Let me reconsider. Maybe bug 3 is the HDFC line 253: `[TRANSFER & partyName.contains("^By") , partyName.replace("^By ", "")]`. If partyName = "By Rajesh" → replace "^By " → "Rajesh". Works. Unless the format is "By-Rajesh" or "By: Rajesh".

I'm not confident HDFC has bug 3. Let me reconsider other banks. The complaint: "transfers whose narration opens with a direction word ahead of the name." Let me look for a rule where a leading direction word is handled with sep_by taking the WRONG index (off-by-one).

Let me reconsider the YES bank UPI rules lines 96-99:
- 96: `^UPI/[0-9]+/From:` & CREDIT → py_extract(desc, "From:(.*)/To", index=0). For credit (received), the sender is after "From:" up to "/To". Works.
- 97: `^UPI/[0-9]+/From:` & DEBIT → py_extract(desc, "/\\s?To:(.*)/", index=0). For debit (sent), the recipient is after "To:" up to "/". Works.

These look correct.

Let me look at the BOB bank fully — it has `^TO TRANSFER-` handling.

THAT's the bug! The account type check `"SAVINGS"` (with trailing S) never matches because the actual value is "SAVING". So the salary sub-tag rule NEVER fires for HDFC! That matches "stopped picking up the salary sub-tag it always had" — a regression where someone changed "SAVING" to "SAVINGS".

Wait, but let me verify the accountType values used. Controller line 106: `if accountType == "SAVING":` — so SAVING. And the categorize functions are called with accountType. So accountType is "SAVING". HDFC line 124 checks `"SAVINGS"` → never true → rule dead → no SALARY sub-tag from this rule. 

But wait — does SalaryDetection.py then detect it? SalaryDetection requires partyName non-empty. For a bare numeric description, partyName is empty (no name extracted by HDFC for a pure-numeric description). So SalaryDetection skips. So no SALARY tag. Matches complaint!

So bug 2 = HDFC line 124: `accountType=="SAVINGS"` → `accountType=="SAVING"`. That's the "boundary" — a typo in a string constant. Hmm, but is that a "structural offset"? It's more a string mismatch. But the maintainer said "boundaries and structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules." A wrong constant in an otherwise-correct rule fits "otherwise-correct rule with a fault."

Actually, this is much better than widening the regex! Because:
- The `^[0-9]{6}$` exact match is fine (salary code is 6 digits).
- The real bug is `"SAVINGS"` vs `"SAVING"`.
- Fixing `"SAVINGS"` → `"SAVING"` makes the rule fire for 6-digit numeric TRANSFER credits in SAVING accounts → SALARY. No currently-correct row changes (the rule was dead). And it doesn't over-match long numeric codes.

This is clearly the right fix. Let me verify other HDFC accountType checks: line 125 uses `"CURRENT"` (correct, matches Controller? Controller doesn't show CURRENT but it's a valid type). Line 126 `accountType != "OVER_DRAFT"`. So only line 124 has the `"SAVINGS"` typo. 

Let me also double-check: is there any place that passes "SAVINGS" (with S)? Controller uses "SAVING". Let me grep for SAVINGS vs SAVING across the codebase to confirm the convention.

steps.message: I've identified the five faults. Let me verify the capture-group behavior for the ICICI direction-word bug and confirm there are no tests/CI commands.
steps.reasoning_content:
Confirmed. The convention is `"SAVING"` (no S). HDFC line 124 is the ONLY place using `"SAVINGS"` (with S). That's the bug. Fix `"SAVINGS"` → `"SAVING"`.

Now wait — but the salary narration is "a bare numeric code and nothing else". With the fix, line 124 fires for a 6-digit numeric TRANSFER credit in SAVING → SALARY. But the salary detection (SalaryDetection.py) ALSO runs after and might reassign. Actually line 124 sets subcategory SALARY, then SalaryDetection sees salaryDetect=True and extends. Fine. The fix restores the salary sub-tag.

But hold on — is the salary narration exactly 6 digits? The complaint says "bare numeric code." With the rule `^[0-9]{6}$`, only exactly-6-digit codes match. If the user's salary code is 6 digits, the fix (correcting SAVINGS→SAVING) makes it work. The complaint "stopped picking up" = regression from the SAVINGS typo. The `^[0-9]{6}$` was presumably always there and worked before the typo. So the salary code is 6 digits, and the regression is the SAVINGS typo. 

So bug 2 fix: HDFC line 124 `"SAVINGS"` → `"SAVING"`. This is clearly the right, minimal fix and doesn't risk over-matching. 

Now let me reconsider bug 1 (cheque) once more in light of this. Both bugs 1 and 2 are in HDFC and are "dead rule" bugs (contradiction/typo making the rule never fire). The maintainer's "structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules" fits both: line 100 has len(15) vs pattern(16) contradiction; line 124 has SAVINGS typo. Both make the rules dead.

Wait — but is the SAVINGS thing really an "offset/boundary"? It's a typo. Hmm. But it does make an otherwise-correct rule never fire. The maintainer said "Expect the faults to sit on boundaries and structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules." The SAVINGS typo is arguably a "boundary" (string-equality boundary that excludes everything). I'll accept it.

Actually, wait. Let me reconsider bug 1 again. Is it possible the bug is also a typo-like issue rather than the len/pattern contradiction? Let me re-examine line 100. The pattern is `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` and length is 15. 11+5=16≠15. This is a clear off-by-one contradiction. The fix: align them. As argued, fix len 15→16 (since pattern implies 16).

But wait — could the intended format be 15 = 11 zeros + 4 digits? `[0]{11}[0-9]{4}` = 15. But the pattern says `[0-9]{5}`. Hmm. Or 15 = 10 zeros + 5 digits, pattern should be `[0]{10}[0-9]{5}`. 

The maintainer says "long all-numeric string." Both 15 and 16 are "long." I'll fix the length to 16 to match the pattern (the pattern is the more deliberate part: 11 zeros + 5 digit cheque number). Actually, HDFC cheque numbers are 6 digits typically. Hmm. 11 zeros + 5 digits = the last 5 are the cheque number? Or 10 zeros + 6 digit = 16? 

I really can't be 100% sure. But the contradiction must be resolved. Let me think about it differently: which fix is safer re "don't change currently-correct rows"? Currently the rule is DEAD (matches nothing). So NO row currently gets CHEQUE_DEPOSIT from this rule. Currently, a 15-char or 16-char zero-padded numeric remark credit → TRANSFER (default). 

- Fix A (len 16): 16-char "0000000000012345" → CHEQUE_DEPOSIT. 15-char ones → still TRANSFER.
- Fix B (pattern `[0]{10}[0-9]{5}`, len 15): 15-char "000000000012345" → CHEQUE_DEPOSIT. 16-char → still TRANSFER.

The complaint: the user's remark is "a long all-numeric string." If it's 16 chars, fix A. If 15 chars, fix B. 

Hmm. The pattern `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` — if I trust the pattern (it's the more specific/structural part), the format is 11 zeros + 5 digits = 16, and the length check (a simple number) has the typo. Fix A.

I'll go with Fix A: len.eq(15) → len.eq(16). This trusts the regex pattern as the source of truth.

Hmm, but actually, let me reconsider. There's a subtle point: `remark.str.contains("^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}", case=False)` — `contains` with `^` anchor matches at start. For a 16-char remark "0000000000012345", `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` matches the first 16 chars = whole string. Good. For a longer remark (17+), it'd match the first 16 and the len check (16) would fail. Fine. So Fix A makes exactly-16-char zero-padded remarks → CHEQUE_DEPOSIT. Good.

OK let me also reconsider bug 5 (HDFC line 98 shift). Let me re-examine. Actually, wait — ICICI has the same reversal/bounce pattern (lines 49-50) but with shift(1) for both (correct). HDFC line 98 uses shift(2) for description (bug), line 99 uses shift(1). So HDFC is the buggy one. The maintainer reproduced "offsetting credit immediately after its matching debit recorded as bounce rather than reversal" — HDFC. Fix line 98: description shift(2) → shift(1).

Let me now also double-check: are there other reversal rules in HDFC that the change might conflict with? Line 25 sets REVERSAL for descriptions containing "REVERSAL"/"REV" keywords. That's independent. Line 98 is the offsetting-credit heuristic. Changing line 98's description shift to 1: for a credit immediately after a matching debit (same desc, contains IMPS/NEFT/UPI/RTGS) → REVERSAL. Previously (shift 2) it required the desc to match the row 2 back, which is wrong. 

But wait — could changing line 98 to shift(1) cause it to now match rows that were previously correctly REVERSAL via line 25? No, line 25 is separate (keyword-based). Line 98 only fires for offsetting credits. And np.select picks the FIRST true condition in the transactionCategory list. Line 98 is at position ~ in the category conditions (lines 11-101). Line 25 (REVERSAL) is at line 25 in the file, which is BEFORE line 98. So for a row matching both line 25 (keyword) and line 98 (offsetting), line 25 wins → REVERSAL. Fine. For a row matching line 99 (AUTO_PAYMENT_BOUNCE, shift 1, no keyword) and line 98 (with keyword, shift 1 after fix), line 98 is first → REVERSAL. Good, that's the fix.

But also — does line 98 after fix potentially steal rows from line 99 that should be AUTO_PAYMENT_BOUNCE? Line 98 requires the description to contain IMPS|NEFT|UPI|RTGS. Line 99 has no such requirement. So a genuine bounce (offsetting credit, same desc, NO payment keyword) → line 98 no match → line 99 → AUTO_PAYMENT_BOUNCE. Preserved. Good.

And the previously-"REVERSAL"-via-shift(2) rows: were there any? Line 98 with shift(2) matched credit rows whose desc == row-2-back's desc AND amount==shift(1) AND type shift(1)==DEBIT AND contains keyword. That's a weird combo. After fix, those rows need desc==shift(1). If they had desc==shift(2) but != shift(1), they lose REVERSAL → maybe become AUTO_PAYMENT_BOUNCE (line 99, if desc==shift(1)? no, desc != shift(1) so line 99 no) → default TRANSFER. Could that break a currently-correct REVERSAL? 

Hmm. Is a row with desc==shift(2) (2 back) but desc != shift(1) (immediate prev) a "correct reversal"? The maintainer says the correct reversal is the immediate-neighbor (shift 1) case. The shift(2) case is the bug, not a correct reversal. So no currently-CORRECT row is broken. The maintainer's own framing supports this. 

I'm confident in all 5 fixes. Let me also verify there are no tests to run. Let me check for a test setup or README.

steps.message:
All five edge-case faults fixed. Each was a dead/off rule — an otherwise-correct rule with one boundary or offset wrong — so no row that currently labels correctly is shifted.

**`categorizationapp/BankScripts/HDFC.py`**
- Line 100 (cheque deposit): `remark.str.len().eq(15)` contradicted the `^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` pattern (16 chars), so the rule never matched and a long all-numeric credit reference fell through to TRANSFER. Fixed length to `eq(16)`. *(mango_saver #1)*
- Line 124 (salary sub-tag): `accountType=="SAVINGS"` is the only place in the codebase using the plural; the controller and every other rule pass `"SAVING"`, so the rule never fired. Fixed to `"SAVING"`. *(mango_saver #2)*
- Line 98 (offsetting-credit reversal): `description.eq(description.shift(periods=2))` compared the credit against the row two back while `amount`/`type` used `shift(1)`, so an immediate debit→credit reversal matched the `AUTO_PAYMENT_BOUNCE` rule (line 99) instead. Aligned description to `shift(periods=1)`. *(maintainer-reproduced #5)*

**`categorizationapp/BankScripts/ICICI.py`**
- Line 147 (bill transfer): for the 4-field `BIL/INFT/<numeric>/<name>` narration (`count("/").eq(3)`), `sep_by_(description,2,3)` lifted the numeric reference. Fixed to `(3,4)` to take the final name field. *(auditlens #4)*
- Line 159 (TRFR transfer): `py_extract(description, "TRFR (TO|FROM):(.*)", index=0)` returns capture group 1 — the direction word `TO`/`FROM` — not the name. Verified via `str.extract`; group 1 is the direction word, group 2 (index=1) is the name. Fixed `index=0` → `index=1`. *(rk_ontheroad #3)*

Both files compile cleanly. No common-path rule was touched, so rows already labeling correctly are unchanged.
